questions.question: Why should hardware be included in a cybersecurity threat model?

options.label: A
options.text: Because hardware can no longer be assumed to be inherently secure and trusted.
options.rationale: Modern cybersecurity must consider that the underlying hardware itself may contain vulnerabilities, malicious modifications, or untrusted components.

options.label: B
options.text: Because software vulnerabilities no longer exist in modern systems.
options.rationale: Software vulnerabilities still exist; the point is that hardware can also be part of the attack surface.

options.label: C
options.text: Because hardware is always easier to patch than software.
options.rationale: Hardware is usually harder to patch or replace once deployed, which makes hardware-level security issues especially serious.

options.label: D
options.text: Because hardware attacks only affect performance, not security.
options.rationale: Hardware attacks can affect confidentiality, integrity, availability, trust, and system behavior.

questions.hint: Think about whether the physical foundation of a system can always be trusted.

questions.question: What is the risk of treating hardware as automatically trusted?

options.label: A
options.text: Security analysis may ignore attacks or vulnerabilities originating from the hardware layer.
options.rationale: If hardware is blindly trusted, designers may fail to detect or defend against hardware Trojans, malicious IPs, compromised supply chains, or insecure debug features.

options.label: B
options.text: The system will become immune to software attacks.
options.rationale: Trusting hardware does not make software secure or immune to exploitation.

options.label: C
options.text: The system will automatically encrypt all data.
options.rationale: Encryption requires explicit security mechanisms; it does not happen just because hardware is trusted.

options.label: D
options.text: The system will stop using embedded components.
options.rationale: Embedded systems still rely heavily on hardware components; the issue is whether those components are trustworthy.

questions.hint: A bad assumption can hide an entire class of attacks.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the modern view of hardware security?

options.label: A
options.text: Hardware must be analyzed as a possible source of vulnerabilities, not just as a trusted foundation.
options.rationale: Modern cybersecurity recognizes that hardware can be compromised, misdesigned, maliciously modified, or exposed through low-level interfaces.

options.label: B
options.text: Hardware is outside the scope of cybersecurity.
options.rationale: Hardware is part of the computing system and can directly affect security.

options.label: C
options.text: Hardware security only matters for military systems.
options.rationale: Hardware security matters in consumer, industrial, automotive, medical, and embedded systems as well.

options.label: D
options.text: Hardware attacks are impossible if the software is updated.
options.rationale: Software updates may not fix vulnerabilities rooted in physical design, fabrication, debug interfaces, or supply-chain compromise.

questions.hint: The hardware layer is part of the attack surface.

questions.question: Which example best matches a hardware-security concern?

options.label: A
options.text: A malicious modification inserted into an integrated circuit during design or manufacturing.
options.rationale: A malicious change in hardware, such as a hardware Trojan, is a typical hardware-security threat.

options.label: B
options.text: A typo in a user interface label.
options.rationale: A UI typo is not normally a hardware-security issue.

options.label: C
options.text: A weak password chosen by a user.
options.rationale: Weak passwords are an authentication and user-security issue, not specifically a hardware-security concern.

options.label: D
options.text: A slow internet connection caused by network congestion.
options.rationale: Network congestion affects performance but is not necessarily a hardware-security attack.

questions.hint: Look for a threat rooted in the physical or design-level components of the system.

questions.question: What is the main reason hardware security is important for information systems?

options.label: A
options.text: Information systems rely on hardware as their underlying execution platform.
options.rationale: If the hardware foundation is compromised or untrusted, higher-level software security mechanisms may also be undermined.

options.label: B
options.text: Hardware completely replaces the need for software security.
options.rationale: Hardware security and software security are complementary; one does not eliminate the need for the other.

options.label: C
options.text: Hardware attacks are always visible to the user.
options.rationale: Hardware attacks can be stealthy and difficult to detect, especially if they are embedded in the design or manufacturing process.

options.label: D
options.text: Hardware components never change after fabrication.
options.rationale: Even if hardware is physically fixed after fabrication, its behavior, configuration, interfaces, and trust assumptions can still create security risks.

questions.hint: If the foundation is not trustworthy, the layers above it may also be affected.
